elements.sent-2411-001: A Doppler radar is a device that employs the Doppler effect to estimate the radial velocity of a moving target at a distance.
elements.sent-2411-002: Traditional radars are based on a classical description of the electromagnetic radiation, but in principle their performance can be improved employing entangled quantum probe states.
elements.sent-2411-003: For target detection, i.e. hypothesis testing, a quantum advantage exists even in the high-noise regime appropriate to describe microwave fields, a protocol known as quantum illumination.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we show a similar advantage also for a quantum Doppler radar operating in presence of thermal noise, whereas so far a quantum advantage was shown in the noiseless scenario or in lidars operating at optical frequencies with negligible thermal noise.
elements.sent-2411-005: Concretely, we quantify the radar performance in terms of the quantum Fisher information, which captures the ultimate precision allowed by quantum mechanics in the asymptotic regime.
elements.sent-2411-006: We compare a classical protocol based on coherent states with a quantum one that uses multimode states obtained from spontaneous parametric downconversion.
elements.sent-2411-007: To ensure a fair comparison we match the signal energy and pulse duration.
elements.sent-2411-008: We show that a 3dB advantage is possible in the regime of small number of signal photons and high thermal noise, even for low transmissivity.
elements.sent-2411-009: Quantum technologies seek to take advantage of genuine quantum effects for increased performances, ideally beating any classical counterpart.
elements.sent-2411-010: While the field is growing and steadily advancing towards concrete applications, adapting theoretical ideas to nonidealities of the real world remains challenging.
elements.sent-2411-011: From a physical perspective, quantum optics is one of the cornerstones of modern quantum technologies; indeed, many tasks in which the electromagnetic field is used as a probe, or as the carrier of information, show the potential for a quantum advantage.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this regard, quantum metrology~\cite{Giovannetti2011,Polino2020 and quantum sensing~\cite{Pirandola2018 are among the most promising applications, including quantum-enhanced protocols for, e.g., interferometry~\cite{Demkowicz-Dobrzanski2015a , spectroscopy~\cite{Dorfman2016 , superresolution~\cite{Tsang2019a and imaging~\cite{Genovese2016,Defienne2024 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: One of the sensing tasks that has attracted more interest from physicists and engineers in the past decade is remote detection of targets, leading to ideas for quantum lidar and radar applications.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this context, the most paradigmatic and studied protocol, known as quantum illumination (QI)~\cite{Lloyd2008,Tan2008,Shapiro2009 , concerns the detection of a weakly reflecting target in a noisy thermal environment: a signal beam entangled with an idler leads to increased success probability with respect to any classical beam.}
elements.sent-2411-015: After the first proposal, a lot of efforts have been devoted to concretize this protocol.
elements.sent-2411-016: There have been experimental demonstrations of quantum advantage in QI at optical frequencies~\cite{Zhang2015e,Xu2021d,Hao2022a .}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, the real challenge is to apply the QI protocol in the microwave band, since in this regime the thermal noise is naturally very high.
elements.sent-2411-018: Theoretical efforts started with Ref.~\cite{Barzanjeh2015 , and new promising design for microwave receivers have been proposed recently~\cite{Shi2024e,Angeletti2023,Reichert2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Several experiments in the microwave domain have been performed~\cite{Luong2018,Chang2019,Barzanjeh2020 , but only recently a real advantage was obtained at cryogenic temperature~\cite{Assouly2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Progress in these fields has been recently surveyed~\cite{Shapiro2020,Torrome2021,Sorelli2022,Casariego2023,Karsa2024,Torrome2024,Zhang2024x .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Going beyond detecting the presence of a target, classical lidar and radar systems are also used to estimate properties of the target, most notably its distance, i.e. ranging, and its radial velocity via Doppler effect.
elements.sent-2411-022: Assuming negligible losses and thermal noise, range and velocity measurements can be regarded as a unitary estimation problem and thus it is possible to reach the Heisenberg limit, a mean squared error inversely proportional to the number of signal photons, representing a quadratic improvement with respect to the performance of classical strategies.
elements.sent-2411-023: The estimation of range~\cite{Giovannetti2001a,Giovannetti2002,Shapiro2007,Maccone2020b , velocity~\cite{Reichert2022 or both parameters simultaneously~\cite{Zhuang2017b,Huang2021,Reichert2024 has been studied in this noiseless regime, which may only be appropriate for optical frequencies and short distances.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Reaching the Heisenberg limit represents the most dramatic advantage in quantum metrology and sensing, but it disappears asymptotically due to most kinds of noise~\cite{Demkowicz-Dobrzanski2012 , even using the most sophisticated strategies to counteract its effect~\cite{Kurdzialek2023a .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: This applies to most real scenarios, including ranging and velocity estimation in presence of optical losses.
elements.sent-2411-026: Nonetheless, even if an asymptotic quadratic advantage is ruled out by the noise, the precision in estimating parameters can still be enhanced by a constant factor.
elements.sent-2411-027: In the microwave domain, not only losses, but thermal noise is naturally present in real applications.
elements.sent-2411-028: The advantages of quantum strategies for range estimation in this scenario have been analyzed in Refs.~\cite{Zhuang2021,Karsa2021a,Zhuang2022,Cohen2022a .}
elements.sent-2411-029: However, to the best of our knowledge, no studies have addressed a quantum Doppler radar in the microwave domain, considering the effect of thermal noise.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this paper we fill this gap by showing that a 3dB advantage is possible for a Doppler radar, in the same high-thermal noise, high-loss and low-signal-power regime where the original QI protocol is effective.
elements.sent-2411-031: More in detail, in our results we consider the quantum Fisher information (QFI) as a figure of merit for the comparison between a classical strategy employing a coherent state and a quantum strategy using an entangled state, obtained by pulsed spontaneous parametric downconversion (SPDC).
elements.sent-2411-032: For a given quantum state, the QFI captures the maximal amount of information about the parameter which can be extracted if an optimal measurement is implemented, in the limit of many repetitions of the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-033: Crucially, in order to fully describe the Doppler effect, including how it affects the spectrum, one has to deal explicitly with a continuous-frequency description of the involved fields~\cite{Huang2021,Reichert2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Therefore, the problem requires a more involved model than the original QI scenario for target detection, where a simple description of the signal evolution in terms of a single-mode bosonic channel suffices~\cite{Tan2008,Shapiro2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Following the approach of Ref.~\cite{Reichert2022 we perform calculations by treating the continuum field in terms of discrete modes.}
elements.sent-2411-036: In this setting, we deal with the estimation of a parameter that affects the underlying structure of the modes of the electromagnetic field, a task for which specific techniques have recently been developed~\cite{Gessner2023a,Sorelli2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-038: In Sec.~\ref{sec:model we introduce the model, including the discrete-mode description and the two classes of classical and quantum probe states.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In Sec.~\ref{sec:results we explain our approach for comparing the performance of classical and quantum strategies and we show numerical results.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Sec.~\ref{sec:conclusions concludes the main part of the paper with some remarks and discussion of open problems.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Sec.~\ref{sec:methods contains most of the technical details, including an introduction to the theoretical framework for quantum estimation theory and Gaussian quantum systems, as well as more details on the calculation of the QFI in the two cases.}

elements.subsection-2411-005: Figures of merit and comparison scheme
elements.sent-2411-117: We want to compare the precision in the estimation of the Doppler parameter $\mu$ obtainable from the two classes of states defined in previous section.
elements.sent-2411-118: We will refer to the protocol employing a coherent state for the signal beam as classical Doppler radar (CDR), while to the protocol based on SPDC states as quantum Doppler radar (QDR).
elements.sent-2411-119: We make the standard assumption that the idler beam of the QDR configuration is perfectly stored without incurring in any additional noise.
elements.sent-2411-120: While standard, this ideal assumption may be challenging to implement, especially for microwaves~\cite{Torrome2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-121: This comparison is carried out using the QFI as the figure of merit.
elements.sent-2411-122: More details on this quantity and on quantum estimation theory are relegated to Sec.~\ref{subsec:qest .}
elements.sent-2411-123: For understanding the results it suffices to know that the QFI is related to the optimal mean-square error of an estimator $\tilde{\mu}$ in the limit of many repetitions $m \gg 1$ as $ (\Delta^2 \tilde{\mu )_\mathrm{opt \approx (m J[\rho_{\mu ] )^{-1}$; a larger QFI means a lower the estimation error.}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: The QFI only depends on the received quantum state of the radiation, the corresponding error amounts to an optimization over all measurements and classical estimators.
elements.sent-2411-125: While optimal measurements may be hard to implement, and may also require adaptive steps in the estimation procedure~\cite{Barndorff-Nielsen2000 , this quantity sets a fundamental lower bound on the precision that it is physically possible to achieve.}
elements.sent-2411-126: Notice that for the CDR, i.e. a classical state of the field, the optimal measurement saturating the QFI may include detection schemes that could be considered nonclassical, such as photon counting.
elements.sent-2411-127: For the CDR we obtain the following closed-form expression for the QFI \begin{equation}\label{eq:Jc} J_c = \frac{4\eta N_S }{ \mu^2 \left({2 N_B + 1} \right)} \int \! dy {\left( \frac{1}{2}f(y) + y \frac{d f(y)}{d y} \right)}^2,\end{equation} which coincides with the expression obtained in Ref.~\cite{Reichert2022 for $N_B= 0$.}

elements.section-2411-006: Comment on Heisenberg and Schrödinger picture for propagating quantum fields
elements.sent-2411-241: We point out a conceptual subtlety in translating from Heisenberg to Schrödinger picture in the context of quantum optics.
elements.sent-2411-242: These ideas are likely already well-known to experts, but we find it instructive to clearly explain the situation.
elements.sent-2411-243: We consider a linear input-output relation between bosonic creation and annihilation operators (formally, a Bogoliubov transformation): \begin{equation}\begin{split} \vec{\hat{\alpha}}_\mathrm{out} \equiv \begin{bmatrix} \vec{\hat{a}}_{\mathrm{out}} \vec{\hat{a}}^\dag_{\mathrm{out}} \vec{\hat{b}}_{\mathrm{out}} \vec{\hat{b}}^\dag_{\mathrm{out}} \end{bmatrix} = \hat{U}^\dag(\vec{\hat{\alpha}}_\mathrm{in}) \vec{\hat{\alpha}}_\mathrm{in} \hat{U}(\vec{\hat{\alpha}}_\mathrm{in}) = \vec{M}_U \vec{\hat{\alpha}}_\mathrm{in} \equiv \vec{M}_U \begin{bmatrix} \vec{\hat{a}}_{\mathrm{in}} \vec{\hat{a}}^\dag_{\mathrm{in}} \vec{\hat{b}}_{\mathrm{in}} \vec{\hat{b}}^\dag_{\mathrm{in}} \end{bmatrix} \end{split}\end{equation} Note that we use the round brackets in $\hat{U (\vec{\hat{\alpha _\mathrm{in )$ to denote the functional dependence of the operator $\hat{U}$ on the bosonic input operators, so that, e.g., $\hat{U (\vec{\hat{\alpha _\mathrm{out )$ represents a different operator, but with the same functional dependence on the bosonic operators in the round brackets.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-244: Similarly, for the initial state $\rho_0(\vec{\hat{\alpha _\mathrm{in )$ denotes the functional form of the state in terms of the input Bosonic operators.}}}
elements.sent-2411-245: We will always assume that the functions are well-behaved so that $ U^\dag f(X) U = f(U^\dag X U)$ for a unitary operator $U$ (this should always hold for analytical functions expressible as power series, such as the exponential function that defines unitaries).
elements.sent-2411-246: In Schrödinger picture we consider the partition $\mathcal{H _{\vec{\hat{a _{\mathrm{in \bigotimes \mathcal{H _{\vec{\hat{b _{\mathrm{in }$ of the total Hilbert space\footnote{The notation $\mathcal{H _{\vec{\hat{a _{\mathrm{in }$ should be self-explanatory, but formally it means that this is the Fock space built by the corresponding creation operators.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-247: Tracing out the environment modes means tracing over the Hilbert space $\mathcal{H _{\vec{\hat{b _{\mathrm{in }$, so that the accessible state is an operator acting only the Hilbert space $\mathcal{H _{\vec{\hat{a _{\mathrm{in }$ defined by the input modes.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-248: If the initial state $\rho_0(\vec{\hat{\alpha _\mathrm{in )$ is factorized across the bipartition $\mathcal{H _{\vec{\hat{a _{\mathrm{in \bigotimes \mathcal{H _{\vec{\hat{b _{\mathrm{in }$, this procedure corresponds to applying a Gaussian quantum channel on the initial state, an operator acting on $\mathcal{H _{\vec{\hat{a _{\mathrm{in }$ and this description is essentially a Stinespring dilation of the channel.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-249: Now the crucial observation is that, from a physical point of view, in quantum optics the local operators available at the receiver are not $\vec{\hat{a _{\mathrm{in }$, but $\vec{\hat{a _{\mathrm{out }$, thus the relevant Hilbert space partition at the receiver's end is $\mathcal{H _{\vec{\hat{a _{\mathrm{out \bigotimes \mathcal{H _{\vec{\hat{b _{\mathrm{out }$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-250: We want to find the form of the density operator $ \rho_{\mathrm{out (\vec{\hat{a _{\mathrm{out )$ at the receiver: this operator acts on $\mathcal{H _{\vec{\hat{b _{\mathrm{out }$ and it can be used to compute the expectation values of all local operators at the receiver, which we write as $f( \vec{\hat{a _{\mathrm{out )$ for an arbitrary function $f$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-251: We can thus write \begin{equation}\Tr_{\vec{\hat{a}}_{out}} \left[ f( \vec{\hat{a}}_{out}) \Tr_{{\vec{\hat{b}}_{out}}} \left[ \rho_0 (\vec{M}_U^{-1}\vec{\hat{\alpha}}_{out}) \right] \right] = \Tr \left[ f( \vec{\hat{a}}_{out}) \rho_0 (\vec{\hat{\alpha}}_{in}) \right] = \Tr \left[ f( \vec{\hat{a}}_{in}) \hat{U}(\vec{\hat{\alpha}}_in) \rho_0 ( \vec{\hat{\alpha}}_{in}) \hat{U}^\dag(\vec{\hat{\alpha}}_in) \right] = \Tr_{\vec{\hat{a}}_{in}} \left[ f( \vec{\hat{a}}_{in}) \Tr_{{\vec{\hat{b}}_{in}}} \left[ \hat{U}(\vec{\hat{\alpha}}_in) \rho_0 ( \vec{\hat{\alpha}}_{in}) \hat{U}^\dag(\vec{\hat{\alpha}}_in) \right] \right],\end{equation} where from the first to the second line we moved from Heisenberg to Schrödinger picture.
elements.sent-2411-252: Since the identity between the initial and final expressions must hold for any $f$, and since the output Hilbert spaces are isomorphic to the input Hilbert spaces, we deduce that \begin{equation}\rho_{out}(\vec{\hat{a}}_{out}) \equiv \Tr_{{\vec{\hat{b}}_{out}}} \left[ \rho_0 (\vec{M}_U^{-1}\vec{\hat{\alpha}}_{out}) \right] = \Tr_{{\vec{\hat{b}}_{out}}} \left[ \hat{U}(\vec{\hat{\alpha}}_out) \rho_0 ( \vec{\hat{\alpha}}_{out}) \hat{U}^\dag(\vec{\hat{\alpha}}_out) \right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-253: This means that the state at the receiver is formally identical to the Schrödinger picture evolved state, up to an isomorphism between the input and output Hilbert spaces.

elements.section-2411-007: Impact of pump bandwidth on the quantum advantage
elements.sent-2411-254: In the main text we have shown that the QDR can have obvious quantum advantages in the following two scenarios: (a) low noise (both loss and thermal noise) and a large average number of signal photons; (b) high thermal noise and low average number of signal photons.
elements.sent-2411-255: From the approximated QFI of the CDR in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Jcapprox it is clear that the time duration of the signal also affects the accuracy of the radar in measuring the Doppler shift induced by the target.}
elements.sent-2411-256: Generally speaking, a wider spectral bandwidth is related to a worse ability to resolve different frequencies, and thus a lower Doppler measurement accuracy, as quantified by the QFI.
elements.sent-2411-257: Conversely, a narrow bandwidth should give a higher the measurement accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-258: In Fig.~\ref{fig:JqJcratio_vs_bandwidth we study the influence of pulse bandwidth $\sigma_p$, on the quantum advantage of the QDR.}
elements.sent-2411-259: The center frequency of the probe signal is set to $\omega_c = \omega_p/2 = 1010 \mathrm{Hz}$, which corresponds to a signal with a wavelength of $\lambda = 6 \pi \mathrm{cm}$ and the bandwidth $\sigma_p$ spans values from $\omega_c/1000$ to $\omega_c/10$, while $\varepsilon$ is fixed.
elements.sent-2411-260: From these values we compute $N_S$ and $\Delta T$ and put the corresponding values to evaluate $J_c$ for the classical protocol.
elements.sent-2411-261: Fig.~\ref{fig:JqJcratio_vs_bandwidth shows that there is relatively weak increase of the quantum advantage as the pump bandwidth increases, for both scenarios (a) and (b) described above.}
elements.sent-2411-262: In particular, in panel (a) we see that for the scenario (a) the effect is very moderate and can essentially be neglected, confirming the observation made in Ref.~\cite{Reichert2022 for the noiseless $\eta=1$ scenario.}
elements.sent-2411-263: In panel (b), we see that for scenario (b) the enhancement can reach more than 0.25 dB, and thus it is not entirely negligible.
elements.sent-2411-264: However, we also remark that going to a large pump bandwidth may break the narrowband approximation under which the model was derived, a more refined model is needed for a proper analysis.
